--

The before/after anatomy of the flip every successful user describes — and the one-hour experiment that triggers it.
Read enough Claude Code stories and a strange pattern emerges. The successful users almost never describe gradual improvement. They describe a flip — a specific point where the tool stopped fighting them and started working, so cleanly that many can name the week it happened.
The frustrated users describe something equally specific: a loop. Vague request, broken output, “fix it,” differently broken output, “no, the OTHER thing,” escalating frustration, rage-quit. Repeat until the subscription gets cancelled and the angry post gets written.
Same tool. Two completely different realities. And the difference between them turns out to be neither talent nor luck nor prompt wizardry. It’s a one-hour experiment that one group ran and the other didn’t — which makes this the most fixable divide in modern software development.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

reactbd.comAnatomy of the loop
Start with the failure mode, because it’s nearly universal and weirdly standardized.
The “before” workflow looks like this everywhere it appears: a terminal opened in a repository the agent has never been given any context about. No project memory file…
